summary: This advice column features Aunt Pythia and guest Tante Nina answering reader questions on diverse topics: deflecting nosy questions with humor, dealing with workplace sexism, knowing when to end a lukewarm relationship, and whether to list 'ABD' on a resume. The longest and most substantive response addresses professional sexism, offering practical advice about documentation while acknowledging the unfair reality women face. The columnists advocate for resilience, humor, and not letting aggressors undermine confidence. Throughout, the tone balances warmth with directness, encouraging readers to maintain their standards while navigating difficult situations.
key_points: Workplace sexism should be documented thoroughly via personal email records to build a potential legal case if needed
The strategy for dealing with workplace bullies and sexists is to deny them the satisfaction of a reaction - humor and brush-offs work better than showing upset
Historical women like Ruth Bader Ginsburg succeeded through 'grit, excellent results, hard work, and playing dumb, dumb like a fox'
In relationships, being bored after four months is not a good enough standard - better to walk away than waste time
Listing 'ABD' on resumes implies active dissertation work; using it when not actively pursuing the degree looks 'weirdly defensive'
money_quotes: Sexism, like racism, is alive and well.
Don't let these guys win by undermining your confidence, because that is what they are trying to do.
With the little micro-aggressions they are trying to put you in your place.
Jerks and bullies generally give up and go away when they can't get a rise from you.
Laugh, brush them off with humor whenever possible, and remember that success is the best revenge.
How did women of that generation get by? On grit, excellent results, hard work, and playing dumb, dumb like a fox.
You gotta have standards, and being bored is not a good enough one.
key_insight: When facing workplace sexism or other forms of bullying, document everything for protection but don't give aggressors the emotional reaction they seek - success and resilience are the best responses.
